calculatorHTML:
Dew Point Calculator

Dew Point
—
Comfort Level
—

howItWorks.intro: Dew point is the temperature the air must cool to before it can no longer hold all its water vapor and moisture begins to condense. This tool derives it from two readings you already have: the current air temperature and the relative humidity. Because dew point tracks the actual amount of moisture in the air rather than a percentage relative to temperature, it stays meaningful even as the temperature swings through the day.

rules.name: Magnus Formula
rules.explanation: The Magnus approximation, where α combines temperature and relative humidity. It is accurate to within a few tenths of a degree across normal weather conditions.

howItWorks.outro: The Magnus formula is an approximation calibrated for typical atmospheric conditions, so expect it to be within a fraction of a degree of a lab-grade hygrometer. It drifts slightly at extreme cold or near saturation. For everyday comfort, HVAC, and condensation decisions that margin is well inside what matters.

faq.question: What is dew point?
faq.answer: Dew point is the temperature air must cool to before water vapor starts condensing into liquid — the point at which the air becomes fully saturated. If the dew point is 55°F, any surface colder than 55°F will start collecting moisture. Unlike relative humidity, it is an absolute measure of how much water is actually in the air.

faq.question: What is the difference between dew point and relative humidity?
faq.answer: Relative humidity is a percentage of how much moisture the air holds compared to the most it could hold at that temperature, so it changes as the temperature changes even when no water is added or removed. Dew point measures the actual moisture content directly. That is why 90% humidity on a cool 50°F morning feels fine, while 60% humidity on a hot 90°F afternoon feels heavy — the second has a much higher dew point.

faq.question: What dew point feels muggy?
faq.answer: As a rough guide: a dew point below 55°F feels dry and comfortable, 55°F to 60°F feels pleasant, 60°F to 65°F starts to feel sticky, 65°F to 70°F is noticeably muggy, and anything above 70°F feels oppressive and sweaty because your perspiration can no longer evaporate efficiently. Most people notice the shift right around the 65°F mark.

faq.question: Why is dew point a better comfort gauge than humidity?
faq.answer: Relative humidity only tells you the moisture level relative to the current temperature, so a high percentage can be misleading. Dew point gives one absolute number you can read the same way in any season: a 70°F dew point feels oppressive whether the air temperature is 75°F or 95°F. That consistency is why meteorologists lean on dew point to describe how the air actually feels.

faq.question: Can dew point ever be higher than the air temperature?
faq.answer: No. The dew point can equal the air temperature — that happens at 100% relative humidity, when the air is fully saturated and fog, dew, or rain is likely — but it can never exceed it. If your inputs produce a dew point above the temperature, double-check that the humidity figure isn't higher than reality.

faq.question: How do I use dew point to prevent condensation and mold?
faq.answer: Condensation forms whenever a surface — a window, a wall, a cold water pipe, an AC duct — drops below the dew point of the surrounding air. Calculate the dew point of a room, then insulate or warm any surface that sits below it, or lower the indoor moisture with ventilation or a dehumidifier. Keeping indoor dew point below roughly 55°F to 60°F sharply reduces the window sweating and damp corners that let mold take hold.

howWeCalculate.intro: The calculator runs the Magnus-Tetens approximation, the same relationship weather services use to turn a thermometer and hygrometer reading into a dew point. Here is what happens after you press Calculate:
steps: Your temperature is read in and, if needed, converted to Celsius for the formula's math.
An intermediate term α is built from the temperature and the relative humidity: α = (17.27 × T)/(237.7 + T) + ln(RH/100).
That term is fed back through Td = 237.7 × α / (17.27 − α) to solve for the dew point temperature.
The result is converted back to °F and matched to a comfort band — dry, comfortable, sticky, muggy, or oppressive — so the number has plain-language context.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: This is the standard Magnus approximation and is accurate to within a fraction of a degree in ordinary weather. It is fine for comfort, HVAC, and condensation planning; for laboratory or industrial process control, use a calibrated instrument.

whenToUse.intro: Dew point turns two ordinary readings into a single number that tells you how the air will actually behave. It is most useful when comfort, moisture, or condensation is on the line:
scenarios: Judging outdoor comfort before a run, a game, or yard work — a 72°F day with a 68°F dew point will feel far heavier than the temperature alone suggests.
Diagnosing window sweating, damp basements, or musty closets, where surfaces are dropping below the room's dew point and condensing water.
Heading off mold and mildew by checking whether indoor dew point is high enough to keep surfaces persistently damp.
Setting up HVAC and dehumidifiers — targeting an indoor dew point (rather than a humidity percentage) gives a comfort goal that holds as the thermostat changes.
Protecting cold pipes, ductwork, and stored goods from condensation in humid spaces like crawlspaces, garages, and workshops.
Understanding a forecast — pairing the reported dew point with temperature tells you whether fog, frost, or a muggy afternoon is likely.

sections.title: What This Calculator Does
sections.body: Enter the air temperature and the relative humidity, and this tool returns the dew point — the temperature the air would have to cool to for its moisture to start condensing. That single number is one of the most honest descriptions of how the air feels and behaves, because it reflects the real amount of water vapor present rather than a percentage that shifts with the thermometer. Alongside the dew point, the tool tags a comfort level so you know at a glance whether the air will feel crisp, pleasant, or heavy.

sections.title: A Worked Example and Comfort Guide
sections.body: Take a warm afternoon at 25°C (about 77°F) with 60% relative humidity. Running that through the Magnus formula gives a dew point near 16.7°C, roughly 62°F — squarely in the range where the air starts to feel a little sticky. Now hold the humidity steady and the dew point barely moves through the evening even as the temperature falls, which is exactly why it is such a stable comfort gauge. Use this rough scale to read any dew point: below 55°F feels dry and comfortable, 55°F to 60°F is pleasant, 60°F to 65°F turns sticky, 65°F to 70°F is noticeably muggy, and 70°F and up feels oppressive because sweat no longer evaporates well. Most people feel the tipping point right around 65°F.

sections.title: Why Dew Point Beats Relative Humidity
sections.body: Relative humidity is a moving target: 80% humidity at 50°F holds far less actual water than 60% humidity at 90°F, yet the higher number sounds worse. Dew point cuts through that confusion by measuring the moisture itself, giving you one figure that means the same thing in July and January. It is also the number that predicts condensation — any surface colder than the dew point will collect water — which makes it the reading to watch for window sweating, damp walls, and mold, not just personal comfort.

howToSteps.title: Enter the Air Temperature (°F)
howToSteps.description: Type in the current temperature. Use an outdoor reading for weather comfort, or the temperature of the specific room when you are chasing down condensation indoors.

howToSteps.title: Enter the Relative Humidity (%)
howToSteps.description: Add the relative humidity for the same location and moment — from a weather app, a thermostat, or an indoor hygrometer. The two readings must come from the same air for the dew point to be meaningful.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Run the numbers. The Magnus formula solves for the dew point instantly, right in your browser.

howToSteps.title: Read the Dew Point and Comfort Level
howToSteps.description: Check the dew point against the comfort scale. Below 55°F is dry, the 60s trend sticky, and 70°F+ is oppressive — and any surface colder than the dew point is where condensation will appear.

tips: Watch the dew point, not the humidity percentage, when you want to know how the air will actually feel — it is the number that stays honest as the temperature changes.
Keep indoor dew point below roughly 55°F to 60°F to stop windows from sweating and to make damp, mold-prone corners far less likely.
Remember the condensation rule: water forms on any surface colder than the dew point, so insulating or warming a cold window or pipe fixes the drips without lowering room humidity.
Compare two moments by running them side by side — a cool humid morning and a hot afternoon can share a humidity reading but have very different dew points.
Set dehumidifiers and AC to a dew point target rather than a humidity percentage; the comfort goal then holds steady even as the thermostat cycles.
